text: The surgeon uses blunt dissection to remove flimsy adhesions in the abdominal cavity, revealing the gallbladder through a small window in the tissue.

The surgeon encounters dense adhesions likely due to previous cholangitis and cholecystitis. Sharp dissection is used to carefully identify the correct plane, with monopolar energy applied in short bursts to minimize smoke and lateral tissue damage. The dissection is performed close to the gallbladder, ensuring that only adhesions are released while avoiding the colon and duodenum.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to expose the entire gallbladder, which appears thick-walled and edematous. Meticulous dissection releases adhesions between the lateral border of the gallbladder, the liver bed, and the omentum. The dissection area is expanded to expose the common bile duct, with fibrous strands divided sharply and remaining tissue moved away bluntly.

The surgeon divides thick adhesions using cold scissors or low-voltage monopolar energy, applying traction to identify the vascular plane. The dissection focuses on flimsy adhesions between the lower border of the liver, using short bursts of energy to minimize thermal damage.

The surgeon exposes the entire gallbladder and hepatoduodenal ligament area, retracting the duodenum downward. A small incision is made on the fat covering the hepatoduodenal ligament, revealing a vein. Careful dissection is performed to preserve important structures, using energy and gauze to keep the field dry. The artery and vein are clearly visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects the incision cranially and caudally between the gallbladder and omental adhesions, ensuring the incision remains on the peritoneum to avoid damaging the common hepatic duct and hepatic artery. Suction is used to confirm the correct plane, followed by traction and both blunt and sharp dissection to expose the posterior surface of the gallbladder while avoiding medial damage to portal structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, creating an anterior window by incising the peritoneum and using energy to expand the opening. An instrument is passed through the window to facilitate further dissection, ensuring the gallbladder is separated from the liver bed both anteriorly and posteriorly. This technique aids in safely identifying and dissecting around the cystic duct and common bile duct.

The surgeon uses an instrument to display the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD) within the created window, confirming no other structures are present between the liver bed and cystic duct. The cystic duct is milked to ensure no remnant stones are present, revealing its wide diameter. A proline suture is used for intracorporeal ligation at the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, followed by incision of the cystic duct using scissors.

The surgeon observes obstructed bile flowing freely and suctions it out. An infant feeding tube is inserted with pressure and saline, causing fluid to gush out, clearing any sludge. A cholangiogram is performed using CM dye to check the patency of intrahepatic ducts, CBD, and duodenum. The distal stump of the cystic duct is ligated with an endo loop, and the gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed, carefully identifying and adhering to the correct planes to avoid deep penetration into the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon places the removed gallbladder into an endobag, positioning the cystic duct portion towards the bag's opening. Sutures are made in a pursing fashion to secure the bag. A wide drain is placed sub-hepatically to prevent post-operative leaks.
